title: Logging — Forwarder Stopped or CrashLoop on Security-Relevant Hosts

spl:
index=_internal source=*splunkd.log* earliest=-24h
| rex field=_raw "(?i)Stopping all processes|crashed"
| stats count by host
| lookup security_tier_hosts.csv host OUTPUT tier
| where tier IN ("tier0","tier1")
description: Detects universal forwarder or agent stopped states on in-scope systems so logging obligations stay met.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001, ISO/IEC 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes _internal. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We catch universal agent or agent stopped states on -scope systems so logging obligations stay met — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
implementation: (1) Maintain tier lookup; (2) auto-restart playbook; (3) ISO evidence for logging availability. Indexes required: `index=_internal`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk `_internal`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `splunkd` logs.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain tier lookup; (2) auto-restart playbook; (3) ISO evidence for logging availability.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal source=*splunkd.log* earliest=-24h
| rex field=_raw "(?i)Stopping all processes|crashed"
| stats count by host
| lookup security_tier_hosts.csv host OUTPUT tier
| where tier IN ("tier0","tier1")
```

Understanding this SPL

**Logging — Forwarder Stopped or CrashLoop on Security-Relevant Hosts (A.8.15)** — Detects universal forwarder or agent stopped states on in-scope systems so logging obligations stay met.

Documented **Data sources**: `splunkd` logs. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk `_internal`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_internal, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Extracts fields with `rex` (regular expression).
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where tier IN ("tier0","tier1")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table, Single value
knownFalsePositives: Access reviews lag during HR transitions, manager changes, or quarterly review cycles.
